coredata.dc:title: Zn2+ doped TiO2/C with enhanced sodium-ion storage properties
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yong-Ni
Su, Jing
Lv, Xiao-Yan
Long, Yun-Fei
Yu, Hang
Huang, Rong-Rong
Xie, Yong-Chun
Wen, Yan-Xuan
coredata.dc:description: To improve the performance of anatase TiO2 as an anode material for sodium-ion batteries, Zn2+-doped TiO2/C composites are synthesized by a co-precipitation method. The results of XRD, EPR and XPS demonstrate that Zn2+ occupies at the Ti4+ site of TiO2 to form a solid-solution, resulting in an expansion of lattice and an increase of Ti3+ content. The expansion of lattice can enhance the stability of the crystal structure of TiO2. The increase of Ti3+ content can improve the conductivity of TiO2. Therefore, Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C delivers a reversible capacity of 160mAhg−1 with a capacity retention of 96% after 100 cycles at 5C. Even charged/discharged at 10C, this sample still exhibits a reversible capacity of 117mAhg−1, comparing to 86mAhg−1 for TiO2/C. The enhanced electrochemical performances can be ascribed to the improvement of the conductivity and the structural stability of TiO2 due to Zn2+-doping. Therefore, Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C is an attractive anode material of sodium-ion batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: B. Microstructure
E. Batteries
E. Electrodes
Sodium-ion batteries
Anatase TiO2
Zinc doping

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Full XRD patterns and (b) 101 peak of the prepared samples.
SEM images for TiO2/C (a, c) and Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C (b, d).
N2 isotherm adsorption/desorption curves and the corresponding pore-size distributions: (a) TiO2/C and (b) Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C.
EPR signals of TiO2/C and Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C.
Charge/discharge curves and cycle performances of TiO2/C and Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C at 5C.
(a) Charge/discharge curves and (b) rate performance of TiO2/C and Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C at different C rates.
Cyclic voltammetry curves of TiO2/C and Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C at the scan rate of 0.2mVs−1 between 0.01V and 2.5V.
Electrochemical impedance spectra and the equivalent circuit of Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C and TiO2/C.
Lattice parameters of TiO2/C and Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C.
Pore parameters of TiO2/C and Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C.
Peak positions (eV) of XPS for Zn2+ doped TiO2/C.
Parameters of the impedance spectra for Zn2+ doped TiO2/C.

head.ce:title: Zn2+ doped TiO2/C with enhanced sodium-ion storage properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Colleges and Universities Key Laboratory of Novel Energy Materials and Related Technology, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Colleges and Universities Key Laboratory of Novel Energy Materials and Related Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The New Rural Development Research Institute, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The New Rural Development Research Institute, Guangxi University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To improve the performance of anatase TiO2 as an anode material for sodium-ion batteries, Zn2+-doped TiO2/C composites are synthesized by a co-precipitation method. The results of XRD, EPR and XPS demonstrate that Zn2+ occupies at the Ti4+ site of TiO2 to form a solid-solution, resulting in an expansion of lattice and an increase of Ti3+ content. The expansion of lattice can enhance the stability of the crystal structure of TiO2. The increase of Ti3+ content can improve the conductivity of TiO2. Therefore, Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C delivers a reversible capacity of 160mAhg−1 with a capacity retention of 96% after 100 cycles at 5C. Even charged/discharged at 10C, this sample still exhibits a reversible capacity of 117mAhg−1, comparing to 86mAhg−1 for TiO2/C. The enhanced electrochemical performances can be ascribed to the improvement of the conductivity and the structural stability of TiO2 due to Zn2+-doping. Therefore, Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C is an attractive anode material of sodium-ion batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) have been considered as a promising source to meet the needs of large-scale grid energy storage because of their abundance and low cost [1–3]. Despite these merits of SIBs, their large-scale applications were limited by the technical barriers in cathode materials, anode materials and electrolytes [1–4]. Therefore, carbon, alloy, Ti-based compounds, metal oxides/sulfides and organic compounds have been investigated as anode materials for SIBs [5,6]. In view of the successful applications in lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), titanium dioxide (TiO2) has been investigated as a promising anode material for sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) due to its safety, cost, environmental friendliness and chemical stability [7–24]. For instance, porous anatase TiO2/C delivered ~255.98mAhg−1 at 1/3C and 84mAhg−1 at 10C [14]. TiO2/C nanofibers exhibited 164.9mAhg−1 at 2000mAg−1 [18]. However, Chen et al. [25], Deng et al. [26] and Zhu et al. [27] reported that TiO2 has the intrinsically low Li-ion diffusivity (∼1×10−15 cm2 s−1) and low electronic conductivity (∼10−13 Scm−1) derived from the huge band gap of ∼3.2eV [28], which severely lead to its inferior rate capabilities in lithium ion batteries. the Na-ion volume is 2.4 times greater than that of the Li ion, meaning that Na+ intercalation into anatase TiO2 is more difficult than that of Li+. Moreover, the activation phenomenon in the discharge–charge process [29] shows that the Na+ intercalation into TiO2 is still kinetically hindered, especially at high rates [14,30]. For example, the DC electrical conductivity of anatase TiO2 with oxygen vacancies (B-TO, 4.3×10−4 Scm−1) is more than 4 orders of magnitude higher that of anatase TiO2 without oxygen vacancies (W-TO, 2.1×10−8 Scm−1), and the specific capacity of BTO (115mAhg−1) is twice that of WTO (~50mAhg−1) at 10C [31]. These results show that the conductivity is a limiting factor for the rate performance of anatase TiO2. So, many efforts including carbon coating [9–11,13,15,17–20,24] and controlling its morphology [7,10,14,16,18,19,21–24] and size [8,11–13,20] were used to improve the conductivity of anatase TiO2. Recently, doping with heterogeneous ions has been investigated as another effective approach to enhance the conductivity of TiO2, and various heterogeneous atoms such as Sn [32], Nb [33,34], Fe [35], Ni [36], Mo [37], B [38], N [39,40] and S [41] were used to increase the conductivity and then improve the rate performance of TiO2. For example, Sn-doped TiO2 gave a capacity of 103mAhg−1 at 5Ag−1 [32]. Nb doped anatase TiO2 exhibited 108.8mAhg−1 at 5C [33]. Fe-doped mesoporous anatase TiO2/C delivered 198mAhg−1 at 2Ag−1 [35]. Ni2+ doped anatase TiO2 still maintained 123mAhg−1 after 2000 cycles at 5Ag−1 [36]. Clearly, heterogeneous atom doping is an effective approach to improve the rate performance of TiO2, but the roles of doping ion need to be further investigated.

More recently, Pang et al. [42] and Varadharajan et al. [43] reported that Zn2+ doping can result in a lattice expansion and the formation of Ti3+. The expansion of lattice and the formation of Ti3+ are useful to improve the lattice stability and the conductivity of TiO2, which are advantageous in enhancing its rate performance [32–41]. Thus, we infer that Zn2+ doping can also improve the rate performance of TiO2. Hence, Zn2+ doped TiO2/C was prepared and characterized in this work. The synthesized Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C delivers a capacity of 160mAhg−1 after 100 cycles at 5C (1.68Ag−1) and 117mAhg−1 at 10C (3.35Ag−1), exhibiting good cycling stability and rate performance. Therefore, Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C is good candidate for SIBs with high performance.

Zn2+ doped TiO2/C was prepared by a co-precipitation using ZnSO4 (AR, Beijing Chemical Reagent Co. LTD, Beijing, China) and Ti(SO4)2 (96.0%, Beijing Chemical Reagent Co. LTD, Beijing, China) as raw materials and yeasts (Yichang Angel Yeast Co., Ltd, Yichang, China) as template and carbon source. The synthetic method has been described in our previous study [14]. First, 50mL ZnSO4-Ti(SO4)2-HCl solution (Zn2+-Ti4+1molL−1) were added to 50mL yeast emulsion being cultivated in 50mL glucose solution (4wt%) at 36°C for 30min, and stirred continually for 3h. Second, a white precipitate was obtained by adjusting the pH value of the mixture solution to 8–10 using ammonia solution (NH4OH, 25–28%) and keeping statically for 18h. Third, the resultant precipitate was centrifuged and washed with deionized water to remove the soluble ion, and dried by a freeze drying. Finally, Ti1-x Zn x O2/C powders were obtained by sintering the dried precipitate at 450°C for 3h in air.

The compositions of the prepared samples were measured via a ICP 6300 (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Massachusetts, USA). The crystal structure of Ti1-x Zn x O2/C powders was identified by a Rigaku D/Max 2500V diffractometer (Rigaku Corporation, Tokyo, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation, and 10wt% standard silicon powder was added to correct the error in XRD experiments. The morphologies of the powders were observed with a field-emission scanning electron microscopy (S4800, Hitachi Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). The N2 isothermal adsorption/desorption curves were measured with a V-Sorb 2800 series analyzer (Gold APP Instruments, Wuhan, China). The specific surface area and pore size distribution of the prepared samples were calculated by BET and BJH methods, respectively. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were performed with ER200D-SRC (Bruker Corporation, Billerica, German) and ESCALAB 250X (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Massachusetts, USA), respectively. A Flash 2000 elemental analyzer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Massachusetts, USA) was used to measure the carbon content of the prepared sample. The DC electrical conductivity was measured by a direct volt-ampere method (ST2722, Suzhou Jingge Electronic Co., LTD, Suzhou, China), in which disk samples were contacted by a four-point probe.

The electrochemical behaviors of the prepared samples were measured with a CR2032 cell assembled in an argon-filled glove box. The CR2032 cell contains a working electrode, a sodium foil anode, 1molL−1 NaPF6 EC/DMC (1:1 by volume) electrolyte and Whitman glass microfiber filter (Grade GF/D) separator. To prepare working electrode, the prepared sample, binder (a mixture of CMC and SBR with a weight of 1:1) and super P were mixed with a mass ratio of 80:10:10 in NMP and then coated on a copper foil collector. After dried under vacuum, the working electrode was punched and weighted its mass. The thickness of the mixture on the Cu foil was ~100µm, and the surface density of the prepared samples was 1.3–1.6mgcm–2. The assembled CR2032 cells were charged and discharged by CC-CV mode at different C rates at 25°C between 0.01 and 2.5V (vs. Na/Na+) on a Neware CT-4008 battery testing system (Neware Technology Limited, Shenzhen, China), where 1C rate was 335mAg−1. Current densities and specific capacities were calculated using the mass of the prepared sample in the working electrode. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were carried out with a three-electrode cell (Lithium Battery Cell 990-00344, Gamry Instruments, Warminster, USA) on an electrochemical workstation (PCI4/750, Gamry Instruments, Warminster, USA), where sodium foil was used as the counter and reference electrode. CV experiments were tested between 0.01 and 2.5V at 0.2mVs−1 at 25°C. EIS experiments were measured from 105 Hz to 0.01Hz at 25°C.

To study the effects of Zn2+ doping, TiO2/C and Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C were prepared, respectively. The compositions of both samples measured by ICP are Ti0.993O2/C and Ti0.942Zn0.064O2/C, which are close to the composition of the designed compounds. XRD, SEM, N2 isotherm adsorption/desorption, EPR and XPS were used to identified the changes of the structures and morphologies due to Zn2+ doping.

Fig. 1 gives the XRD patterns of TiO2/C and Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C. The XRD patterns in Fig. 1(a) can be indexed as anatase TiO2 (JCPDS No. 21-1272) and no peaks related to zinc compounds are observed. We can speculate that Zn2+ is introduced into anatase phase matrix to form a solid-solution Ti1-x Zn x O2 [32–37], because the six-coordinated Ti4+ can be substituted by six coordinated Zn2+ [43]. As shown in Fig. 1(b), the 101-peak in shifts to the lower angle, suggesting that the crystal cell is expanded after Zn2+ doping. The lattice parameters of the prepared samples were calculated by WPF (Whole Pattern Fit) function of MDI Jade 6.0 and presented in Table 1. As seen in Table 1, Zn2+ doping expands the unit cell of TiO2, because the ionic radius of Zn2+(0.740Å) [44] is larger than that of Ti4+(0.605Å) [44]. The results from Fig. 1 and Table 1 demonstrate that Zn2+ doping can expand the interlayer spacing in TiO2. Thus, we can believe that Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C with larger unit cell and interlayer spacing should have better cycle-stability, because larger interlayer spacing is more conducive to enhance the Na+ storage performance [36,37]. On the other hand, the content of carbon of TiO2/C and Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C measured by an elemental analyzer is 1.50 and 1.65wt%, respectively. However, no peaks related the crystallized carbon can be observed in Fig. 1(a) due to its amorphous structure or its content too low to be detected by XRD [14]. The residual carbon from the pyrolysis of the yeast cells can facilitate electron conduction [9–15,17,18].

SEM images in Fig. 2(a) and (c) show that all samples have similar morphology, which consist of nanoparticles (Fig. 2(b) and (d)), indicating that Zn2+ doping has little effect on the particle growth [33,36].

The effects of Zn2+ on the porous structure and the special surface area were further investigated using a N2 isotherm adsorption/desorption technology. Fig. 3 gives isotherm adsorption/desorption curves and the pore diameter distribution curves (inlet) of TiO2/C and Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C. The isotherm adsorption/desorption curves in Fig. 3 have a type IV isotherm with an H3 hysteresis loop, suggesting that both samples are a typical mesoporous material [14]. The BET surface area, average pore size and pore volume of Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C and TiO2/C are presented in Table 2. As seen in Table 2, the BET surface area, average pore size and pore volume of Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C are close to that of TiO2/C, indicating that Zn2+ doping has little effect on the porous structure of TiO2. In general, the large surface area and mesoporous structure of TiO2/C and Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C can greatly facilitate the penetration of the electrolyte for the promotion of Na+ diffusion and improve electron transport to reduce electrode polarization [14,45,46].

Many researchers have reported that metal doping in TiO2 would result in the formation of oxygen vacancies in TiO2 [32–37], which are co-existent with Ti3+ [47,48]. Therefore, EPR was used to investigate the effects of Zn2+ doping on the formation of Ti3+ and oxygen vacancies in TiO2, because EPR is very sensitive to detect the unpaired electron of Ti3+. As shown in Fig. 4, the EPR curves of both samples have a pair of peaks related to Ti3+, indicating that there are oxygen defects and Ti3+ in Ti0.96Zn0.06O2/C and TiO2/C. Furthermore, the peak intensity of Ti0.96Zn0.06O2/C is larger than that of TiO2/C, demonstrating that Zn2+ doping is beneficial to form Ti3+, leading to an increase in Ti3+ content.

XPS was carried out to further identify the chemical oxidation of the prepared TiO2/C and Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C. As shown in Fig. 5, TiO2/C and Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C have similar XPS spectra. The peaks of Zn2p, Ti (LMM), O (KLL), Ti2s, O1s, Ti2p, C1s, Ti3s and Ti3p can be observed and labeled in Fig. 5(a) and (b), respectively. The XPS peak positions of Ti, Zn and O are listed in Table 3. The peaks located at ~464 and ~458eV is related to Ti2p1/2 and Ti2p3/2, respectively, which are the characteristic for Ti4+ [49,50]. The small peak at ~457.6eV is agreement with the characteristic peak of Ti3+ in Ti2O3 [50], further proving that Ti3+ exists in both TiO2/C and Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C.

To further identify the effects of Zn2+ doping on the electric conductivity of the prepared samples. The DC electric conductivity measured by a four-point probe is 5.74×10−5 Scm−1 for Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C. In comparison to the result of the undoped TiO2/C (electric conductivity 6.23×10−7 Scm−1), the conductivity of Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C is more than 2 orders of magnitude higher due to a larger Ti3+ content in Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C.

Fig. 6 gives the charge/discharge curves and the cycle performance of TiO2/C and Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C at 5C. As seen in Fig. 6(a), the charge-discharge curves of TiO2/C gradually move to the lower capacity with the increase of the cycle numbers. However, the charge-discharge curves of Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C show a good overlap at different cycles (Fig. 6(b)), indicating that Na+ extraction/insertion processes in Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C are more reversible than that in TiO2/C. As shown in Fig. 6(c), Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C gives a discharge capacity of 160mAhg−1 with a capacity retention of 96% after 100 cycles at 5C, while pure TiO2/C only gives a discharge capacity of 146mAhg−1 with a capacity retention of 82% after 100 cycles at 5C. The improved cycle stability can be attributed to the enhanced structural stability and the conductivity by Zn2+ doping.

Fig. 7 gives rate performance of Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C and TiO2/C at different current densities. The capacities of both samples decrease with increasing the charge/discharge currents (Fig. 7(a)). The difference in capacity becomes progressively more obvious at higher rates (Fig. 7(b)). Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C delivers a discharge capacity of 235, 217, 189, 178, 159 and 117mAhg−1 at 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5 and 10C, respectively. However, TiO2/C only exhibits a discharge capacity of 235, 189, 169, 145, 117 and 86mAhg−1 at 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5 and 10C, respectively. The capacity of Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C at 1.68Ag−1 (5C) is better than that of Sn-doped TiO2 nanotubes (150mAhg−1 at 1.25Ag−1) [32], Nb-doped TiO2 nanoparticles (108mAhg−1 at 1.65Ag−1) [33] and Mo-doped TiO2 (108mAhg−1 at 1.68Ag−1) [37]. However, it is worse than that of Fe-doped mesoporous TiO2/amorphous carbon composites (198mAhg−1 at 2Ag−1) [35] and Ni-doped TiO2 nanotubes (123mAhg−1 at 5Ag−1) [36]. The improved rate performance of Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C can be ascribed to the enhanced inherent conductivity and lattice stability due to Zn2+ doping [33,35–37], which are consistent with the results from Figs. 1–4, and can also be further confirmed by the CV and EIS measurements as presented in
Figs. 8 and 9.

Fig. 8 gives the CV curves of TiO2/C and Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C after three cycles at 25°C and 0.2mVs−1. The peaks at ~0.55V and ~0.85V are due to the redox of anatase TiO2 [32,36,37]. The reduction peak potential the of TiO2/C is closed to that of Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C, and the oxidation peak potential of TiO2/C is larger than that Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C, suggesting that Zn2+ doping can reduce the potential separations between the anodic and cathodic peaks of TiO2. The peak currents of Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C are larger than that of TiO2/C. The results of CV demonstrate that Zn2+ doping can enhance the electrode kinetic of TiO2 [32,33,35–37].

Furthermore, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to investigate the Na+ intercalation in two samples, which were tested after 3 cycles at 0.2C. The Nyquist plots of TiO2/C and Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C (Fig. 9) consist of a compressed semicircle in the middle frequency and an inclined line in the low frequency. The intercept on the Z/-axis in the high frequency represents the ohmic resistance of the cell, while the semicircle was mainly related to the charge-transfer resistance occurring at the electrolyte/electrode interface, including the transfer resistance of electron and Na+. The straight line is named as the Warburg impedance associated with Na+ diffusion in the active particles [33]. The Nyquist plots in Fig. 8 show that the semicircle for the Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C is smaller than that for TiO2/C. The corresponding equivalent circuit is given in the inset of Fig. 9, where Re is the solution resistance, Rs is the resistance for Na+ transfer through the SEI film, Rct is the charge transfer resistance, and ZW is the Warburg impedance. C1 and C2 represent the corresponding capacitance. The impedance parameters are calculated and summarized in Table 4. TiO2/C and Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C show similar Re and Rs, but TiO2/C shows much higher Rct than that of Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C, indicating the electronic conductivity of TiO2 is effectively improved due to Zn2+ doping. Na+ diffusion coefficient can be calculated by using the following equation:
(1)

N
a
+

R
2

2

2
A
2

4

2
where R is the gas constant (8.314Jmol−1 K−1), T is the absolute temperature (298.15K), n is the number of electrons transferred per molecule during the reaction (n=1), F is the Faraday constant (96,500Cmol−1), A is the surface area of the electrode (1.54cm2), C is the concentration of Na+(1.7×10−2 molcm−3), and σ is the Warburg factor being calculated from the slope of the
Z
/
∝
ω
− 1 / 2
. The calculated
D
N
a
+
of Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C (1.33×10–13 cm2 s−1) is more than one order of magnitude higher than that of TiO2/C (1.56×10–14 cm2 s−1). The EIS analysis shows that Zn2+ doping levels can effectively lower charge transfer resistance and increase the sodium diffusion coefficient. The lower charge transfer resistance and the larger Na+ diffusion coefficient mean that Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C have better performance, which is agreement with the results in Figs. 6 and 7.

Zn2+ doped TiO2/C were synthesized by a co-precipitation process followed by a thermal treatment in air. The results of XRD, EPR and XPS show that Zn2+ is introduced into TiO2 crystal to form a solid-solution, leading to an expansion of lattice and an increase of Ti3+ content. When used as an anode material for SIBs, Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C exhibits a capacity of 160mAhg−1 with a capacity retention of 96% after 100 cycles at 5C. Even charged/discharged at 10C, Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C still delivers a capacity of 117mAhg−1 at 10C, and TiO2/C only gives 86mAhg−1. The improved rate performance and cycling stability can be mainly ascribed to the enhanced electrical conductivity and structural stability due to Zn2+ doping. These results show that Ti0.94Zn0.06O2/C is a promising anode for SIBs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant Nos. 51564002, and 21606055).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.ceramint.2017.05.063.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Na-ion batteries, recent advances and present challenges to become low cost energy storage systems
Electrode materials for rechargeable sodium-ion batteries: potential alternatives to current lithium-ion batteries
Na-ion battery anodes: materials and electrochemistry
Self-organized amorphous TiO2 nanotube arrays on porous Ti foam for rechargeable lithium and sodium ion batteries
Nanocrystalline anatase TiO2: a new anode material for rechargeable sodium ion batteries

High electrochemical performances of microsphere C-TiO2 anode for sodium-ion battery
High cyclability of carbon-coated TiO2 nanoparticles as anode for sodium-ion batteries
Nanocrystalline TiO2 (B) as anode material for sodium-ion batteries
Ultrafast sodium storage in anatase TiO2 nanoparticles embedded in carbon nanotubes
Yeast bio-template synthesis of porous anatase TiO2 and potential application as an anode for sodium-ion batteries
Anatase TiO2@C composites with porous structure as an advanced anode material for Na ion batteries
On the electrochemical encounter between sodium and mesoporous anatase TiO2 as a Na-ion electrode
Carbon-coated rutile titanium dioxide derived from titanium-metal organic framework with enhanced sodium storage behavior
Electrospun TiO2/C nanofibers as a high-capacity and cycle-stable anode for sodium-ion batteries
Graphene-modified TiO2 microspheres synthesized by a facile spray-drying route for enhanced sodium-ion storage
Graphene-rich wrapped petal-like rutile TiO2 tuned by carbon dots for high-performance sodium storage
Hierarchical rutile TiO2 with mesocrystalline structure for Li-ion and Na-ion storage
Hierarchical tubular structures constructed from rutile TiO2 nanorods with superior sodium storage properties
Phases hybriding and graphene-like TiO2 for high-performance Na-ion batteries
Anatase TiO2-reduced graphene oxide nanostructures with high-rate sodium storage performance
Titanium‐based anode materials for safe lithium‐ion batteries
Green energy storage materials: nanostructured TiO2 and Sn-based anodes for lithium-ion batteries
Ti-based compounds as anode materials for Li-ion batteries
Ionothermal synthesis of black Ti3+-doped single-crystal TiO2 as an active photocatalyst for pollutant degradation and H2 generation
Unfolding the mechanism of sodium insertion in anatase TiO2 nanoparticles
Anatase TiO2 nanoparticles for high power sodium-ion anodes
Black anatase titania with ultrafast sodium-storage performances stimulated by oxygen vacancies
Sn-doped TiO2 nanotubes as superior anode materials for sodium ion batteries
Niobium doped anatase TiO2 as an effective anode material for sodium-ion batteries
Nb-doped rutile TiO2: a potential anode material for Na-ion battery
High performance sodium storage of Fe-doped mesoporous anatase TiO2/ amorphous carbon composite
Improved sodium-ion storage performance of TiO2 nanotubes by Ni2+ doping
Mo-doped gray anatase TiO2: lattice expansion for enhanced sodium storage
Boron-doped anatase TiO2 as a high-performance anode material for sodium-ion batteries
Carbon dots supported upon N-doped TiO2 nanorods applied into sodium and lithium ion batteries
Nitrogen-doped ordered mesoporous anatase TiO2 nanofibers as anode materials for high performance sodium-ion batteries
Self-supported nanotube arrays of sulfur-doped TiO2 enabling ultra-stable and robust sodium storage
Synthesis and modification of Zn-doped TiO2 nanoparticles for the photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline
Enhanced visible light photocatalytic activity of Ag and Zn doped and co-doped TiO2 nanoparticles
Crystal Structure Characterized by Powder Diffraction
Porous electrode materials for lithium-ion batteries-how to prepare them and what makes them special
Origin of magnetic moments in defective TiO2 single crystals
One-pot synthesis of bicrystalline titanium dioxide spheres with a core-shell structure as anode materials for lithium and sodium ion batteries
High electrochemical performances of microsphere C-TiO2 anode for sodium-ion battery
